sanskrit_devanagari:
एषा तेऽभिहिता सांख्ये बुद्धिर्योगे त्विमां श‍ृणु ।
बुद्ध्या युक्तो यया पार्थ कर्मबन्धं प्रहास्यसि ॥ ३९ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
eṣā te 'bhihitā sāṅkhye buddhir yoge tv imāṁ śṛṇu |
buddhyā yukto yayā pārtha karma-bandhaṁ prahāsyasi || 39 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical; vedabase uses 'सांख्ये' with anusvara, while some editions render 'साङ्ख्ये' with explicit ङ्ख conjunct — both are valid renderings of the same phonological form sāṅkhye; we adopt the anusvara form per vedabase and holy-bhagavad-gita; bhagavad-gita.us uses 'sankhye' in plain transliteration)
exact (IAST text body identical; plain transliteration 'esa te abhihita sankhye buddhir yoge tv imam srnu')
exact (academic critical edition; Bhg_02.039 = MBh_06,024.039). Body text identical.

anvaya.meaning: in / from sāṅkhya (analytical knowledge of the self)
you will cast off / be released from

translations.verbatim_quote: Thus far I have described this knowledge to you through analytical study. Now listen as I explain it in terms of working without fruitive results. O son of Pṛthā, when you act in such knowledge you can free yourself from the bondage of works.

Hitherto, I have explained to you Sānkhya Yog, or analytic knowledge regarding the nature of the soul. Now listen, O Parth, as I reveal Buddhi Yog, or the Yog of Intellect. When you work with such understanding, you will be freed from the bondage of karma.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: According to the Nirukti, or the Vedic dictionary, sāṅkhya means that which describes phenomena in detail, and sāṅkhya refers to that philosophy which describes the real nature of the soul. And yoga involves controlling the senses. Arjuna's proposal not to fight was based on sense gratification.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.39 Partha, O son of Prtha (Arjuna); esa, this; buddhih, wisdom, the Knowledge which directly removes the defect (viz ignorance) that is responsible for sorrow, delusion, etc., constituting mundane existence; abhihita, has been imparted; te, to you; sankhye, from the standpoint of Self-realization, with regard to the discriminating knowledge of the supreme Reality. Tu, but; srnu, listen; imam, to this wisdom which will be imparted presently; yoge, from the spandpoint of Yoga.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads 2.39 as the META verse that explicitly names the two threads of the chapter and pivots from the first to the second. 'Sāṅkhye' is the standpoint of Self-realisation (jñāna-mārga, knowledge as direct remover of ignorance); 'yoge' is the standpoint of Karma-yoga (action with detachment, performed for the sake of the Lord). Crucially, Shankara reads these as TWO DIFFERENT DISCIPLINES — not two phases of the same path. Knowledge alone removes ignorance; action alone does not. But action, performed without attachment, prepares the conditions in which knowledge can arise. The verse is therefore a structural pivot, not just a topic shift: Krishna is naming what kind of teaching he has been giving (sāṅkhya) and what kind he is about to give (yoga of action), and is explicitly asking Arjuna to receive them as related but distinct.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.39 'Sankhya' means 'intellect,' and the truth about the Atman, which is determinable by the intellect, is 'Sankhyam'. Concerning the nature of the self which has to be known, whatever Buddhi has to be taught, has been taught to you in the passage beginning with, 'It is not that I did not exist' (II.12) and ending with the words, 'Therefore, you shall not grieve for any being' (II.30).
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja agrees with Shankara on the verse's META function but reads the relationship between sāṅkhya and yoga differently. For Ramanuja, the two are not parallel mārgas but sequential moments: knowledge of the self (sāṅkhya, 2.12-2.30) is the necessary precondition; the disposition of mind required to perform actions in the light of that knowledge is what Krishna calls yoga here. He explicitly cites the boundaries: sāṅkhya is everything from 2.12 ('It is not that I did not exist') to 2.30 ('therefore you should not grieve'). What follows is yoga. The verse is therefore a TABLE OF CONTENTS for the chapter — Krishna naming the structure of what he has taught and what he will teach. Ramanuja also reads 'karma-bandham prahāsyasi' as the destination promise: the bondage to be cast off is the bondage of saṁsāra, of which karma is the active mechanism.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: In this verse, Krishna concludes the jnana yoga which he has just taught. Sankhya means 'that by which true knowledge of things (vastu tattvam) is perfectly (samak) revealed (khyayate).' In other words, sankhya means a perfect process of knowing. The intelligence (esa) resulting from that process has been explained. Now hear about the intelligence resulting from bhakti yoga (yoge) which will now be explained.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Vishvanatha gives 2.39 a third reading: 'yoge' here is bhakti-yoga, not karma-yoga. He etymologises sāṅkhya as 'that by which the truth of things is perfectly revealed' — a process of knowing. The verse pivots from the process of knowing to the disposition of devotion. This is the Gaudiya reading: the doctrinal pivot named in 2.39 leads not just into karma-yoga (Shankara) or yoga-as-precondition-for-knowledge (Ramanuja) but into bhakti as the active discipline. Three traditions, three readings of what 'yoge' specifically refers to — but all three agree the verse is structurally pivotal.

disagreements_among_translators.word: sāṅkhye buddhir yoge tv imāṁ śṛṇu

positions.rendering: described this knowledge through analytical study; now listen as I explain it in terms of working without fruitive results
explained to you Sānkhya Yog, or analytic knowledge regarding the nature of the soul; now listen as I reveal Buddhi Yog, or the Yog of Intellect
two distinct standpoints (jñāna and karma-yoga), not two phases — knowledge removes ignorance directly; action prepares the ground
two sequential moments — sāṅkhya (2.12-2.30) is precondition; yoga is the disposition of mind for acting in its light
yoga here is bhakti-yoga; the verse pivots from the process of knowing to the disposition of devotion

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Three major commentarial traditions agree on the META function of 2.39 (Krishna names the chapter's two threads and pivots from the first to the second) but disagree on what the second thread is. Shankara reads 'yoge' as karma-yoga proper (action with detachment, parallel to but distinct from jñāna). Ramanuja reads 'yoge' as the disposition required to act in the light of self-knowledge (sequential, not parallel). Vishvanatha reads 'yoge' as bhakti-yoga (devotion as the active discipline). The translators flatten this into 'analytical study + working without fruitive results' (Prabhupada) or 'Sānkhya Yog + Buddhi Yog' (Mukundananda). The flattening loses the doctrinal-traditional weight of the verse. For the engineering layer: the META function is what survives — Krishna is naming the structure of his teaching, naming sāṅkhya AND yoga as two distinct intellectual disciplines that the practitioner needs both of.

literal_meaning: This wisdom told to you is from sāṅkhya (analytical knowledge); now hear it from yoga. Joined to this wisdom, son of Pṛthā, you will cast off the bondage of action.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The chapter's META verse — Krishna explicitly NAMES the two doctrines (sāṅkhya and yoga) and pivots from the first to the second. The structural hinge of the chapter: 2.11-2.30 was sāṅkhya (analytical knowledge of the imperishable self); 2.31-2.38 was the worldly-frame argument and equanimity-seed; 2.39 onwards is yoga. The verse is rare for being explicitly meta — Krishna is not arguing a position; he is telling Arjuna what kind of teaching he has been giving and what kind he is about to give. The commentarial tradition agrees on the META function but splits on 'yoge': Shankara reads karma-yoga (parallel discipline to jñāna); Ramanuja reads disposition for acting in the light of self-knowledge (sequential moment); Vishvanatha reads bhakti-yoga. Across all three, the verse authorizes the chapter thesis's three-thread reading. 'Karma-bandham prahāsyasi' is the promise: the discipline named here, practiced, ends saṁsāric bondage. The verse is the doctrinal authorization for treating the chapter as having two distinct intellectual projects, both required, neither reducible.
